* No import issues: install.packages("ISLR2"), then data(Carseats)


---
class: left

.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## The CRISP-DM Cycle

.panelset[
.panel[.panel-name[Cycle]
.pull-left[
<svg viewBox="-20 -20 300 300" width="100%">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrowC" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8"
            refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#686868"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- Kreisbogen -->
  <circle cx="130" cy="130" r="110" fill="none"
          stroke="#b3b2b2" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="8,4"/>
  <!-- Phasen-Ellipsen -->
  <ellipse cx="130" cy="25"  rx="55" ry="18" fill="#D50006"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;font-weight:bold;fill:white;text-anchor:middle" x="130" y="29">Business Understanding</text>
  <ellipse cx="228" cy="80"  rx="48" ry="18" fill="#7d0a52"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;font-weight:bold;fill:white;text-anchor:middle" x="228" y="84">Data Understanding</text>
  <ellipse cx="228" cy="175" rx="48" ry="18" fill="#502479"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;font-weight:bold;fill:white;text-anchor:middle" x="228" y="179">Data Preparation</text>
  <ellipse cx="130" cy="235" rx="48" ry="18" fill="#502479"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;font-weight:bold;fill:white;text-anchor:middle" x="130" y="239">Modeling</text>
  <ellipse cx="32"  cy="175" rx="48" ry="18" fill="#7d0a52"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;font-weight:bold;fill:white;text-anchor:middle" x="32" y="179">Evaluation</text>
  <ellipse cx="32"  cy="80"  rx="48" ry="18" fill="#686868"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;font-weight:bold;fill:white;text-anchor:middle" x="32" y="84">Deployment</text>
  <!-- Daten-Ellipse Mitte -->
  <ellipse cx="130" cy="130" rx="38" ry="28" fill="#b3b2b2"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;font-weight:bold;fill:#333;text-anchor:middle" x="130" y="127">Daten</text>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:8px;fill:#333;text-anchor:middle" x="130" y="139">(Zentrum)</text>
</svg>
]
.pull-right[
<strong>Not a linear process — an iterative cycle!</strong>
<br><br>
.blockquote[
"CRISP-DM stresses that the sequence is not rigid – moving back and forth between phases is the norm, not the exception."
.tr[`r Citet(bib, "provost_data_2013")`, S. 27]
]
]
]

.panel[.panel-name[Module Context]
.pull-left[
.small[
| Phase | Today |
|-------|-------|
| **Business Understanding** | ✅ Find underperformers |
| **Data Understanding** | ✅ Lab 1: EDA |
| **Data Preparation** | ✅ Lab 2: Feature Engineering |
| **Modeling** | ✅ Lab 2 & Demo |
| **Evaluation** | ✅ Lab 3 |
| **Deployment** | ➡ Managerial Memo |
]
]
.pull-right[
<br><br>
The module covers all phases — from initial Business Understanding through to final Deployment in the form of a Managerial Memo.
]
]
]

???
* CRISP-DM = Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining
* Provost & Fawcett use it as a framework for all chapters
* Important: In practice, 70–80% of time is spent in Data Understanding & Preparation


---
class: left

.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## Problem Translation: From Business Speak to Data Speak

.panelset[
.panel[.panel-name[The Translation]

.pull-left[
### Business Speak
> *"Find the underperforming branches!"*

.small[
Is this a **classification problem**?
* Underperformer: Yes/No?

`r fa('times-circle')` **No!** Why not?

* Binary classification requires a defined threshold
* Who draws the line? Arbitrary!
* We lose information about the **degree** of underperformance
]
]

.pull-right[
### Data Speak
`r fa('check-circle')` **Scoring / Ranking** via Regression

.small[
We estimate the **conditional mean**:

$$E[\text{Sales} \mid \mathbf{x}] = \hat{y}$$

An **underperformer** is a branch with a strongly **negative residual**:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i \ll 0$$

→ *"The branch sells much less than the market benchmark would predict."*
]
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[Visualization]

```{r}
#| label: problem-translation
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4
#| out-width: "90%"
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
n <- 60
x <- runif(n, 50, 200)
y <- 8 - 0.025 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 1.2)

df <- tibble(
  Price    = x,
  Sales    = y,
  Residual = lm(Sales ~ Price)$residuals
) |>
  mutate(
    Typ = case_when(
      Residual < -1.8 ~ "Underperformer",
      Residual >  1.8 ~ "Outperformer",
      TRUE            ~ "Normal"
    )
  )

df %>% 
ggplot(aes(x = Price, y = Sales)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = Typ, size = Typ), alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#502479",
              se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_segment(
    data = filter(df, Typ == "Underperformer"),
    aes(xend = Price, yend = Sales - Residual),
    color = "#D50006", linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  annotate("text", x = 170, y = 2.3,
           label = "Negative residual\n= Underperformer",
           color = "#D50006", size = 3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Underperformer" = "#D50006",
                                "Outperformer"   = "#502479",
                                "Normal"         = "#b3b2b2")) +
  scale_size_manual(values  = c("Underperformer" = 4,
                                "Outperformer"   = 3,
                                "Normal"         = 2)) +
  labs(x = "Price", y = "Sales (thousands of units)",
       color = NULL, size = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

]
]

???
* The statistical core: regression as benchmark model
* Residual = deviation from "what the market would expect"




.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## (Business) Data Science and Programming

.pull-left[

<br>

"You don't need to be a software engineer to work as a business data scientist. But you do need to be able to read and write in a high-level *scripting* language - in other words, flexible code that can be used to describe recipes for data analysis."
.tr[— .small[Matt Taddy (2019).]]
]
.pull-right[
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '80%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','taddy.jpg.webp'))
```
]






---
class: left

.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## Data Understanding: Coding as a Basic Requirement

.panelset[
.panel[.panel-name[Competence]

.fig.upper-right[
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '50%'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','Programming_Thinking_(SteveJobs).PNG'))
```
]

.fig.lower-left[
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '50%'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','Programming_Quote_(Hawking).PNG'))
```
]

.quellePanURL2[Bildquellen: [ilovecoding.org](https://twitter.com/ilovecodingorg/status/844202136980389888),  [imrananees.blogspot.com](https://imrananees.blogspot.com/2019/10/basic-computer-programming-essential.html).]

]
.panel[.panel-name[Benchmark]
Example: Harvard Business School

* **Elective Modules „Data Science for Managers" (DSM)**
.small[
* DSM1: „Although few HBS MBA students will be actively engaged in
coding post-graduation, the course faculty believe that having **some
exposure to basic, hands-on coding and techniques** will allow our
students to **more effectively manage and interact with data
scientists**. DSM1 will not delve deeply into technical details, but will
require students to engage with some code." (Emphasis not in the original)

* DSM2: „[..] allows students to build a deeper understanding of **how
data and analytics can complement judgment for managerial
decision making**. The course builds on concepts learned in DSM1
and is specifically suited for students who want to continue their
career at companies such as technology companies, where data
collection, aggregation, and analysis permeates the entire
organization." (Emphasis not in the original)
]
]
]


---
class: left

.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## There is no "best" Data Science software. But good reasons for `r fa('r-project')`

.panelset[
.panel[.panel-name[Popularity]

```{r}
#| label: whyR
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '100%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','whyR.svg'))
```

.quelle[Own illustration.]
]
.panel[.panel-name[Taddy (2019)]
* "[R] is an open-source high-level language for data analysis. R is used widely throughout industry, government, and academia."

* "*No language is the best for all purposes.*

* "For the fundamentals of statistical analysis, R is tough to beat [.]"

* "However, in practice, a business data scientist will need to be able to read and make small changes in a bunch of languages—Python, Julia, SQL, and perhaps Scala or Java [...]. A good trick is to learn one language well (probably R) and then pick up what you need as you go."


`r fa('r-project')` Tool-Agnostic Approach at FFHS!
]
]



???

* March 2024: Rank 6 in the [PYPL Popularity Ranking](https://pypl.github.io/PYPL.html)
* For statistical applications, R still leads, especially in academia.
* R as a starting point — languages can be combined depending on the field of application.


---
class: left

.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## `r fa('r-project')`-chitecture: An Open-Source Ecosystem for Data Analysis
.pull-left[
```{r}
#| label: r-chitektur
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '100%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','R-chitektur_(Field_2012_S63).PNG'))
```
]
.pull-right[
* Base `r fa('r-project')`
  * the "engine" / "operating system"
  * local installation via CRAN (Comprehensive R Archive Network)
* Libraries (also called packages)
  * the "apps"
  * extend functionality
]


.quelle[Bildquelle: `r Citet(bib, "field_discovering_2012")`, S. 63.]


---
class: left

.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## RStudio as a "User Interface"

.panelset[
.panel[.panel-name[R vs. RStudio]
.small[
* The `r fa('r-project')` user interface is minimal by design:
  * On opening, only the **`r fa('r-project')` Console** appears.
  * Input can also be written in `r fa('r-project')` script files (.R).
  
* RStudio is an integrated development environment (IDE)...
  * ...available as a free desktop version.
  * ...also available as a cloud version ([posit Cloud](https://posit.cloud/)) with some limitations.
  * ...enabling more user-friendly use of `r fa('r-project')` (e.g. GUI, context menu, autocompletion, etc.)
]
]
.panel[.panel-name[Quick Tour]
.small[
* RStudio has several panels with additional tabs: Script (for documentation), Console (for direct commands), Environment (data), Packages, Plots, etc.
]
<p align="center"><iframe width="650" height="350" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/SdMPh5uphO0?si=0RqBc7jmHesShdsF" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" allowfullscreen></iframe></p>

]
]


---
class: left

.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## If `r fa('r-project')` is a language, we speak a dialect: the Tidyverse

.panelset[
.panel[.panel-name[Data Project Workflow]
```{r}
#| echo: false
xfun::from_root('img','Session1','DataScience_Workflow.png') |>
  knitr::include_graphics()
```
.quellePanURL[Own Illustration.]
]
.panel[.panel-name[Tidyverse]
```{r}
#| echo: false
xfun::from_root('img','Session1','tidyverse_(storybench).png') |>
  knitr::include_graphics()
```
.quellePanURL[Source: [Frigard (2017)](https://www.storybench.org/getting-started-with-tidyverse-in-r/)].
]
.panel[.panel-name[Tidy Data]
```{r}
#| label: img-tidy-format
#| echo: false
#| fig.align: center
#| out.width: '70%'
knitr::include_graphics('https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/6f1ddb544fc5c69a2478e444ab8112fb0eea23f8/91adc/images/tidy-1.png')
```
quellePanURL[Source: [Wickham et al.](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/tidy-data.html)]
`r NoCite(bib,"grolemund_tidy_2017")`
]
.panel[.panel-name[Verbs]
Application-specific functions (so-called "verbs"):
.small[
1. Rows: 
  * `filter()` 
  * `slice()` 
  * `arrange()` 
2. Columns:
  * `select()` 
  * `rename()` 
  * `mutate()` 
3. Groups of rows:
  * `summarise()` 
  * `group_by()`
]

More detail in the script [Data Projects in R](https://ffhs-economicresearch.github.io/ER014/Rmd/PVA1/01_DatenprojekteR.html#1) or in [Chapter 3 of moderndive.com](https://moderndive.com/3-wrangling.html)
`r NoCite(bib,"ismay_statistical_2020-1")`

]
.panel[.panel-name[Pipes]

* Pipes improve code readability:
```{r pipe-img, echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','pipe_(dodona).png'))
```
.quellePanURL[Bildquelle: [Dodona](https://dodona.be/en/activities/2127610019/)]

`r fa('circle-right')` Output of one function as input to the next

* **magrittr** pipe (`%>%`) vs. **native pipe** (`|>`, from R 4.1.0)

* Convention for ER014: magrittr pipe (shortcut Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + M)

]
]



---
class: left

.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## Reproducibility with R Projects and Quarto

* R Projects
   * Project folder with consistent structure: folders for
     * Data
     * R scripts
     * Quarto files (.qmd)
     * ...
  * Documentation with README.md at each folder level
  * A project is only set up once the .Rproj file has been created in the root directory.
  * Navigation using `xfun::from_root()`
  * Template in FFHS Moodle: `R-Projektvorlage.zip`

* Quarto as an integrated publication tool
  * Code and text in one file
  * Automated document generation in various output formats



---
class: left

.blockquote[CRISP-DM & R-Tooling]

## Why QuartoR/Markdown?


.panelset[
.panel[.panel-name[Purpose]
* Ability to integrate `r fa('r-project')` program code (statistical
analyses) and text (verbal descriptions/explanations) into a
single file

* Reproducibility of research/analyses through documentation

* Easier sharing of analysis results (including documentation)

* Numerous common output formats (.docx, .pptx, .pdf, .html)

* Increased productivity for recurring reports/updates (automation)
]
.panel[.panel-name[Quarto vs. RMArkdown]
```{r}
#| echo: false
knitr::include_url('https://quarto.org/docs/faq/rmarkdown.html',height = '400px')
```
]
.panel[.panel-name[Airbnb Case]
.blockquote[
"At Airbnb, all R analyses are documented in rmarkdown, where code and
visualizations are combined within a single written report. Posts are
carefully reviewed by experts in the content area and techniques used,
both in terms of methodologies and code style, before publishing and
sharing with the business partners."
.tr[
`r Citet(bib,"bion_how_2017")`, S. 7.
]
]
```{r}
#| label: rbnb
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '50%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','Rbnb.PNG'))
```
]
]



---
class: left

.blockquote[RProjects & Quarto]

## `r fa('flask', fill='#D50006')` Lab 1

<div style="border:3px solid #D50006; border-radius:8px; padding:20px 28px;
            background:#fff5f5; margin-top:1em;">
<div style="font-family:sans-serif; font-size:16px; font-weight:bold;
            color:#D50006; margin-bottom:10px;">
`r fa('flask')` Lab 1: R Projects &amp; Quarto — Project structure, data import and first presentation
</div>
<div style="font-size:0.85em; line-height:1.8;">
<strong>Learning objectives:</strong> Set up an R project with clean folder structure · Import data from packages and files · Apply first exploratory commands · Create and render a Quarto document · Use inline R code
<br>
<strong>Datasets:</strong>
<code>ames</code> (Paket <code>openintro</code>) &nbsp;·&nbsp;
<code>autos_(StockerUIBK)_20240414.csv</code> &nbsp;·&nbsp;
<code>buli_raw_2023-09-06.rds</code>
<br>
`r fa('clock')` <strong>30 minutes</strong> &nbsp;·&nbsp;
`r fa('users')` <strong>Individual work</strong>
<br>
`r fa('circle-right')` <strong>Open lab document:</strong>
<code>Session1_Lab1_RProjekte_Quarto.qmd</code>
</div>
</div>
<br>
.center[.small[Continue after the lab → <strong>Debrief: Project structure & data import</strong>]]



---
class: inverse, center, middle

## Teil 4: Labs 2 & 3

.blockquote[Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) · Baseline-Model]

---
class: left

.blockquote[Data Understanding]

## EDA: Visualize first, model second

```{r datasaurus}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| out-width: '65%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
library(datasauRus)
datasaurus_dozen %>%
  ggplot(aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~dataset, nrow = 3) + 
  theme_light(base_size = 9) +
  labs(title = "Datasaurus Dozen: alle Datensätze haben gleiche deskriptive Statistiken!",
       subtitle = "Gleicher Mittelwert, gleiche SD, gleiche Korrelation — aber völlig unterschiedliche Verteilungen")
```

.center[`r fa('circle-right')` **EDA first** — always! Statistics lie. Visualization uncovers.]

???
* Anscombe Quartet is the classic — Datasaurus Dozen is the modern version
* Same means, SDs and correlations with completely different patterns
* Kernbotschaft: Vor jeder Regression EDA durchführen




---
class: left

.blockquote[Lab 2 — Exploratory Data Analysis]

## `r fa('laptop-code', fill='#D50006')` Lab 2

<div style="border:3px solid #D50006; border-radius:8px; padding:20px 28px;
            background:#fff5f5; margin-top:1em;">
<div style="font-family:sans-serif; font-size:16px; font-weight:bold;
            color:#D50006; margin-bottom:10px;">
`r fa('flask')` Lab 2: Data Understanding &amp; EDA mit Carseats
</div>
<div style="font-size:0.85em; line-height:1.8;">

<strong>Learning objective:</strong> Exploratory Data Analysis als ersten CRISP-DM-Schritt durchführen.
Verteilungen verstehen, Zusammenhänge visualisieren, Hypothesen formulieren.

<br>

<strong>Dataset:</strong> <code>ISLR2::Carseats</code> (400 Filialen, 11 Variablen)
&nbsp;·&nbsp; <strong>CRISP-DM-Phase:</strong> Data Understanding

<br>

`r fa('clock')` <strong>20 Minuten</strong> &nbsp;·&nbsp;
`r fa('users')` <strong>Individual or pairs</strong>

<br>

`r fa('circle-right')` <strong>Open lab document:</strong>
<code>PVA1_Lab2_EDA.qmd</code>

</div>
</div>

<br>
.center[.small[Continue after the lab → <strong>Debrief: The Taddy Logic</strong>]]

???
* Goal: students should recognise the right-skewness of Sales
* And the weak price effect in a simple scatter
* Lab-Dokument enthält Hintergrundmaterial zur EDA-Theorie


---
class: left

.blockquote[Lab 2 — Debrief]

## Debrief Lab 2: The Taddy Logic

.panelset[
.panel[.panel-name[What did we see?]

```{r}
#| label: lab1-debrief
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 3.8
#| out-width: '100%'
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(ISLR2)
library(patchwork)
tbl_carseats <- tibble(Carseats)

p1 <- tbl_carseats %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = Sales)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#D50006", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = mean(tbl_carseats$Sales), color = "#502479",
               linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
    labs(title = "Sales — raw data",
         subtitle = paste0("Mean = ", round(mean(tbl_carseats$Sales),1),
                           " | Median = ", round(median(tbl_carseats$Sales),1)),
         x = "Sales (thousands)", y = "Count") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "#D50006", face = "bold"))

p2 <- tbl_carseats %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = log(Sales))) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#502479", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = mean(log(tbl_carseats$Sales)), color = "#D50006",
               linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
    labs(title = "log(Sales) — transformed",
         subtitle = "Approximately symmetric after log transformation",
         x = "log(Sales)", y = "Count") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "#502479", face = "bold"))

p3 <- tbl_carseats %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = Price, y = Sales)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#7d0a52", size = 1.5) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#D50006", se = FALSE) +
    labs(title = "Price vs. Sales",
         subtitle = "Negative trend — but extremely high dispersion",
         x = "Price (USD)", y = "Sales (thousands)") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "#7d0a52", face = "bold"))

p1 + p2 + p3
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[The Theory Behind It]

.pull-left[
**Why does revenue data look like this?**

Revenue follows a **multiplicative process**:

$$\text{Sales}_i = \alpha \cdot \text{Price}_i^{\beta_1} \cdot \text{Adv}_i^{\beta_2} \cdot \varepsilon_i$$

* One USD more advertising at a small branch → large relative effect
* One USD more at a large branch → small relative effect

`r fa('circle-right')` OLS on raw data: **Heteroskedasticity**!
]

.pull-right[
**The solution: Log transformation**

$$\ln(\text{Sales}_i) = \alpha' + \beta_1 \ln(\text{Price}_i) + \varepsilon_i'$$

.blockquote[
"Sales and revenue data exhibit multiplicative noise. Taking logs converts this to additive noise — making OLS appropriate."
.tr[`r Citet(bib, "taddy_business_2019")`, S. 11]
]

`r fa('circle-right')` Coefficients become **elasticities**:
$\beta_1 = -1.2$ → 1% price increase → 1.2% revenue decrease
]
]
]




---
class: inverse, center, middle

## Break `r fa('coffee')`

### 10 minutes


---
class: inverse, center, middle

## Part 5: Modelling & Demo

.blockquote[Descriptive Regression · Complexity · Demo: Multiple Model]


---
class: left

.blockquote[Modelling]

## Models are abstractions...

...and therefore always wrong. But some are more useful than others.

.pull-left[
```{r}
#| label: box-pic
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '70%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','GeorgeEPBox.jpg'))
```
]
.pull-right[

"Since all models are wrong the scientist cannot obtain 
a "correct" one by excessive elaboration. On the contrary 
following *William of Occam* he should seek an economical 
description of natural phenomena. Just as the ability to 
devise simple but evocative models is the signature of the 
great scientist so overelaboration and overparameterization is often the mark of mediocrity."
.tr[
— .small[George E.P. Box [Science and Statistics](https://www-sop.inria.fr/members/Ian.Jermyn/philosophy/writings/Boxonmaths.pdf), S. 792.]]
]

???
* `r Citet(bib, "box_science_1976")`
* Aphorismus: "All models are wrong, but some are useful"
* Transition: today ONLY descriptive (population) — Session 2 brings the inference perspective


---
class: left

.blockquote[Modelling]

## Useful Models in a Business Context

.pull-left[

<br>

"An interpretable model translates raw data into information that is directly relevant to decision making."
.tr[— .small[Matt Taddy (2019).]]
]
.pull-right[
```{r}
#| label: taddy-pic
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '80%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','taddy.jpg.webp'))
```
]


???


---
class: left

.blockquote[Modelling]

## Modelling in Statistics vs. Machine Learning (ML)

.pull-left[

"But whereas statisticians have often focused on model inference -- or understanding the parameters of their models (e.g., testing on individual coefficients in a regression) -- the ML community has been more focused on the single goal of maximizing *predictive performance*. [..] And while there is a recent push to build more transparancy into machine learning, wise practitioners avoid assigning structural meaning to the parameters of their fitted models."
.tr[— .small[Matt Taddy (2019).]]
]
.pull-right[
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '80%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','taddy.jpg.webp'))
```
]

???

Denn: 

* "These models are black boxes whose purpose is to do a good job in predicting a future that follows the same pattern as in past data."

* "ML algorithms learn to predict a future that is mostly like the past."

---
class: left

.blockquote[Modelling]

## Business Data Science & AI Value Creators

.pull-left[

"ML algorithms learn to predict a future that is mostly like the past. [..] So prediction doesn't solve everything. However, structural analysis relies upon having strong prediction capabilities. You need to use domain structure to break complicated questions [..] into a bunch of tasks that can be solved with "dumb" off-the-shelf" ML tools. Good data science is all about figuring out what you can outsource as a prediction task and reserving your statistical and economic efforts for the tough structural questions."


.tr[— .small[Matt Taddy (2019).]]
]
.pull-right[
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| out-width: '80%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
knitr::include_graphics(xfun::from_root('img','Session1','taddy.jpg.webp'))
```
]

???


---
class: left

.blockquote[Modelling]

## Regression as a Descriptive Tool — Population Only!

.pull-left[
### What OLS delivers today

OLS estimates the **conditional mean** of Y given X:

$$\hat{y}_i = \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1 \cdot x_i = E[Y \mid X = x_i]$$

`r fa('circle-right')` This is **not** a causal statement

`r fa('circle-right')` This is **not** a prediction model for new data

`r fa('circle-right')` This is a **descriptive summary** of the relationship in the available data

<br>

### Today: Population (descriptive)

All 400 Carseats branches are our population.

`r fa('circle-right')` In Session 2: What if we only had a **sample**?
]

.pull-right[
```{r reg-pop}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| out-width: '90%'
#| fig-align: 'center'
library(ISLR2)
library(moderndive)
tbl_carseats <- tibble(Carseats)
tbl_carseats %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Price, y = Sales)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#7d0a52", size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#D50006",
              se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Carseats: Price vs. Sales",
       subtitle = "OLS line = conditional mean E[Sales | Price]",
       x = "Price (USD)", y = "Sales (thousands of units)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

.small[`r fa('circle-right')` The regression line is the **market benchmark**: what should a branch sell at this price?]
]

???
* Important emphasis: today ONLY descriptive (population)
* Session 2 brings the inference perspective (sample → population)





---
class: left

.blockquote[Lab 3 — Baseline Model]

## `r fa('laptop-code', fill='#D50006')` Lab 3

<div style="border:3px solid #D50006; border-radius:8px; padding:20px 28px;
            background:#fff5f5; margin-top:1em;">
<div style="font-family:sans-serif; font-size:16px; font-weight:bold;
            color:#D50006; margin-bottom:10px;">
`r fa('flask')` Lab 2: Baseline Model — Simple Regression with Carseats
</div>
<div style="font-size:0.85em; line-height:1.8;">

<strong>Learning objective:</strong> Einfaches OLS-Modell als Benchmark schätzen, Koeffizienten
interpretieren, Residuen als Underperformance-Maß verstehen.
Regression als deskriptives Werkzeug (Population).

<br>

<strong>Dataset:</strong> <code>ISLR2::Carseats</code>
&nbsp;·&nbsp; <strong>Model:</strong> <code>Sales ~ Price</code> (Einfachregression als Baseline)

<br>

`r fa('clock')` <strong>15 minutes</strong> &nbsp;·&nbsp;
`r fa('users')` <strong>Individual or pairs</strong>

<br>

`r fa('circle-right')` <strong>Open lab document:</strong>
<code>Session1_Lab2_Baseline.qmd</code>

</div>
</div>

<br>
.center[.small[Continue after the lab → <strong>Debrief: Underfitting &amp; Omitted Variable Bias</strong>]]

???
* R² will be low (~0.15) — this is the didactic point
* Residuals show systematic patterns → making Omitted Variable Bias visible


---
class: left

.blockquote[Lab 3 — Debrief]

## Debrief Lab 3: Underfitting & Omitted Variable Bias

```{r lab2-debrief}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 3.8
#| out-width: '100%'
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(ISLR2)
library(broom)
tbl_carseats <- tibble(Carseats)
mod_simple <- lm(Sales ~ Price, data = tbl_carseats)
r2_simple  <- round(summary(mod_simple)$r.squared, 3)
pts <- augment(mod_simple, data = tbl_carseats)

p_scatter <- ggplot(tbl_carseats, aes(x = Price, y = Sales)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#7d0a52", size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#D50006", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("text", x = 70, y = 15,
           label = paste0("R² = ", r2_simple,
                          "\nPrice explains only ",
                          round(r2_simple*100), "% der Varianz!"),
           color = "#D50006", size = 3, hjust = 0, fontface = "bold") +
  labs(title = "Simple regression: Sales ~ Price",
       x = "Price (USD)", y = "Sales (thousands)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "#D50006", face = "bold"))

p_resid <- ggplot(pts, aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid,
                           color = ShelveLoc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#686868",
             linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Bad"    = "#D50006",
                                "Medium" = "#b3b2b2",
                                "Good"   = "#502479")) +
  labs(title = "Residual plot — coloured by shelf location",
       subtitle = "Systematic pattern = missing variable (ShelveLoc)!",
       x = "Fitted value", y = "Residual",
       color = "ShelveLoc") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "#502479", face = "bold"))

p_scatter + p_resid
```

.small[
`r fa('circle-right')` $R^2$ = `r round(summary(lm(Sales ~ Price, tbl_carseats))$r.squared, 2)` — der Preis allein erklärt nur einen Bruchteil der Umsatzunterschiede. &nbsp; `r fa('circle-right')` Residuen sind nach **ShelveLoc** gefärbt — klares systematisches Muster → **Omitted Variable Bias** (`r Citet(bib, "taddy_business_2019")`)
]

???
* Kernbotschaft: niedrige R² + Muster im Residualplot → Modell unvollständig
* ShelveLoc is a confounder — this motivates the multiple model



---
class: left

.blockquote[Input: Complexity]

## Complexity & "Bad Data Mining"

.pull-left[
### The Overfitting Problem

.blockquote[
"**Discovery without overfit.**  
The challenge of data science is to find real patterns — not to memorize the noise in your sample."
.tr[`r Citet(bib, "taddy_business_2019")`, S. 8]
]

<br>

**What happens when blindly "throwing in all variables"?**

We have 11 variables in Carseats.  
By chance, some will look significant — even if there is no real relationship.

`r fa('circle-right')` **False Discoveries** in High-Dimensional Settings  
`r fa('circle-right')` Solution: FDR (→ Session 2), Cross-Validation (→ ER017)
]

.pull-right[

<svg viewBox="0 0 220 180" width="95%">
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;fill:#686868;
               text-anchor:middle" x="110" y="12">Overfitting: model "connects all dots"</text>
  <!-- Punkte -->
  <circle cx="30"  cy="140" r="5" fill="#D50006"/>
  <circle cx="55"  cy="80"  r="5" fill="#D50006"/>
  <circle cx="80"  cy="120" r="5" fill="#D50006"/>
  <circle cx="105" cy="50"  r="5" fill="#D50006"/>
  <circle cx="130" cy="100" r="5" fill="#D50006"/>
  <circle cx="155" cy="60"  r="5" fill="#D50006"/>
  <circle cx="180" cy="130" r="5" fill="#D50006"/>
  <!-- Overfit-Kurve (wackelig) -->
  <path d="M30,140 C42,40 55,80 80,120 C95,150 105,50 130,100
           C145,130 155,60 180,130"
        stroke="#D50006" stroke-width="2" fill="none"/>
  <!-- Gute Gerade -->
  <line x1="25" y1="125" x2="185" y2="95"
        stroke="#502479" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/>
  <!-- Legende -->
  <line x1="25" y1="165" x2="55" y2="165"
        stroke="#D50006" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;fill:#D50006" x="60" y="169">Overfit (learning noise)</text>
  <line x1="25" y1="178" x2="55" y2="178"
        stroke="#502479" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;fill:#502479" x="60" y="182">Good model (signal)</text>
</svg>

]

???
* Taddy's "High-Dimensional" problem: p ≈ n leads to False Discoveries
* Solution: Model selection, Cross-Validation, Regularisation (Session 2+)
* Today: Manual, thoughtful variable selection


---
class: left

.blockquote[Input: Confounder]

## Confounders & Ceteris Paribus Logic

.panelset[
.panel[.panel-name[The Problem]

.pull-left[
**Should we filter out "Bad ShelveLoc" branches from the dataset?**

Intuition: *"They are different — they disrupt the model."*

<br>

`r fa('times-circle')` **No!** That would be **information loss**.

.blockquote[
"Throwing away data is almost always a mistake. Add the variable as a control instead."
.tr[`r Citet(bib, "taddy_business_2019")`, S. 42]
]
]

.pull-right[
**Solution: Categorical control variable**

ShelveLoc has 3 levels: `Bad`, `Medium`, `Good`

R automatically creates **dummy variables**:

<table style="font-size:0.82em;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;">
<thead><tr>
  <th style="padding:4px 8px;background:#f5f5f5;border:1px solid #ddd;">ShelveLoc</th>
  <th style="padding:4px 8px;background:#f5f5f5;border:1px solid #ddd;text-align:center;">ShelveLocGood</th>
  <th style="padding:4px 8px;background:#f5f5f5;border:1px solid #ddd;text-align:center;">ShelveLocMedium</th>
</tr></thead>
<tbody>
<tr><td style="padding:4px 8px;border:1px solid #ddd;">Bad</td><td style="text-align:center;border:1px solid #ddd;">0</td><td style="text-align:center;border:1px solid #ddd;">0</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#f9f9f9;"><td style="padding:4px 8px;border:1px solid #ddd;">Medium</td><td style="text-align:center;border:1px solid #ddd;">0</td><td style="text-align:center;border:1px solid #ddd;">1</td></tr>
<tr><td style="padding:4px 8px;border:1px solid #ddd;">Good</td><td style="text-align:center;border:1px solid #ddd;">1</td><td style="text-align:center;border:1px solid #ddd;">0</td></tr>
</tbody>
</table>

Reference level = **Bad** (included in the intercept)
]

]

.panel[.panel-name[Ceteris Paribus]

**What does the coefficient of `Price` measure in the multiple model?**

$$\hat{\text{Sales}} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Price} + \beta_2 \cdot \text{Advertising} + \beta_3 \cdot \text{ShelveLocGood} + \ldots$$

`r fa('circle-right')` $\beta_1$ measures the effect of `Price` **holding all other variables constant** (*ceteris paribus*)

<br>

.blockquote[
"Multiple regression does not tell us what happens if we change Price. It tells us what the data shows about Price AFTER removing the influence of all other variables."
.tr[`r Citet(bib, "provost_data_2013")`, S. 147]
]

**In context:** What does 1 USD more advertising bring — controlling for price and shelf location?
]
]

???
* Dummy coding: R does this automatically for factor variables
* Reference level: always first factor level (alphabetical) → "Bad"
* Ceteris Paribus = "all else equal" — the core idea of multiple regression


---
class: left

.blockquote[Live Demo]

## `r fa('display', fill='#D50006')` Demo — Reference

<div style="border:3px solid #502479; border-radius:8px; padding:20px 28px;
            background:#f8f5ff; margin-top:1em;">
<div style="font-family:sans-serif; font-size:16px; font-weight:bold;
            color:#502479; margin-bottom:10px;">
`r fa('display')` Live Demo: Multiple Model in RStudio
</div>
<div style="font-size:0.85em; line-height:1.9;">

<strong>► Step 1:</strong> Estimate multiple model
<code>lm(Sales ~ Price + Advertising + ShelveLoc, data = Carseats)</code>

<strong>► Step 2:</strong> Read regression table
<code>get_regression_table(modell_multipel)</code>

<strong>► Step 3:</strong> Interpret coefficients —
What does <code>ShelveLocGood</code> mean?
How does $\hat\beta_{\text{Price}}$ change vs. baseline model?

<strong>► Step 4:</strong> Residual plot — what else do we see?

<br>

`r fa('circle-right')` <strong>Fully annotated demo script:</strong>
<code>Session1_Demo_MultipleRegression.qmd</code>
(Roadmap for Lecturer, Cheat Sheet for Students)

</div>
</div>

???
* Screen sharing: Demonstrate directly in RStudio
* R² should be significantly higher than simple model (~0.50+)
* Interpret ShelveLocGood coefficient: What does "Good" vs. "Bad" mean in USD?
* Multicollinearity: show cor(Carseats$Income, Carseats$Price) live





---
class: inverse, center, middle

## Part 6: Lab 4 & Outlook


---
class: left

.blockquote[Lab 4 — Performance Mining]

## `r fa('laptop-code', fill='#D50006')` Lab 4

<div style="border:3px solid #D50006; border-radius:8px; padding:20px 28px;
            background:#fff5f5; margin-top:1em;">
<div style="font-family:sans-serif; font-size:16px; font-weight:bold;
            color:#D50006; margin-bottom:10px;">
`r fa('flask')` Lab 4: Performance Mining &amp; Managerial Memo
</div>
<div style="font-size:0.85em; line-height:1.8;">

<strong>Learning objective:</strong> Residuen als Underperformance-Maß nutzen, Underperformer-Ranking
erstellen, Ergebnisse als Manager-Memo formulieren (Translator-Rolle).

<br>

<strong>Dataset:</strong> <code>ISLR2::Carseats</code>
&nbsp;·&nbsp; <strong>CRISP-DM phases:</strong> Evaluation &amp; Deployment

<br>

`r fa('clock')` <strong>15 min. lab + 15 min. group work memo</strong> &nbsp;·&nbsp;
`r fa('users')` <strong>Groups of 3–4</strong>

<br>

`r fa('circle-right')` <strong>Open lab document:</strong>
<code>Session1_Lab4_PerformanceMining.qmd</code>

</div>
</div>

<br>
.center[.small[Continue after the lab → <strong>Debrief: Make or Buy?</strong>]]

???
* Goal: students create the "Wanted List" themselves
* Then transition to the Managerial Memo — this is the translator role live


---
class: left

.blockquote[Lab 4 — Debrief]

## Debrief Lab 4: Make or Buy?

.pull-left[
### The Decision Logic

**Buying data is worthwhile when:**

$$\underbrace{E[\Delta \text{Profit}]}_{\text{better decisions}} > \underbrace{C_{\text{Data}}}_{\text{costs}}$$

**Example:**
* Demographic data: 50,000 EUR/year
* Wrong decision (incorrect branch closure): 500,000 EUR
* P(wrong decision without data) = 30%
* P(wrong decision with data) = 10%

$E[\Delta] = (0.30 - 0.10) \times 500{,}000 = 100{,}000 > 50{,}000$ ✅

.small[`r Citet(bib, "provost_data_2013")`, Kap. 11: "Expected Value Framework"]
]

.pull-right[

<svg viewBox="0 0 240 180" width="95%">
  <defs><style>
    .wscale { stroke: #686868; stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; }
  </style></defs>
  <!-- Waage -->
  <line class="wscale" x1="120" y1="30" x2="120" y2="80"/>
  <line class="wscale" x1="100" y1="20" x2="140" y2="20"/>
  <line class="wscale" x1="120" y1="20" x2="120" y2="30"/>
  <!-- Linke Schale (Dateneinkauf) - leichter -->
  <line class="wscale" x1="120" y1="60" x2="50" y2="75"/>
  <line class="wscale" x1="50"  y1="75" x2="50" y2="105"/>
  <ellipse cx="50" cy="112" rx="42" ry="12" fill="#502479"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;font-weight:bold;
               fill:white;text-anchor:middle" x="50" y="116">Data costs</text>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:8px;fill:#502479;
               text-anchor:middle" x="50" y="132">50,000 EUR</text>
  <!-- Rechte Schale (Fehlentscheidung) - schwerer -->
  <line class="wscale" x1="120" y1="60" x2="190" y2="50"/>
  <line class="wscale" x1="190" y1="50" x2="190" y2="80"/>
  <ellipse cx="190" cy="87" rx="45" ry="12" fill="#D50006"/>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:9px;font-weight:bold;
               fill:white;text-anchor:middle" x="190" y="91">Wrong decision costs</text>
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:8px;fill:#D50006;
               text-anchor:middle" x="190" y="107">100,000 EUR E[Δ]</text>
  <!-- Fazit -->
  <text style="font-family:sans-serif;font-size:11px;font-weight:bold;
               fill:#D50006;text-anchor:middle" x="120" y="155">→ Buying data is worthwhile!</text>
</svg>

]

???
* Expected Value Framework is a central concept from Provost & Fawcett
* Thomas et al.: When data is available to everyone → no competitive advantage (Commodity)


---
class: left

.blockquote[Synthesis]

## Session 1 Synthesis

.pull-left[
### What did we learn?

`r fa('check-circle')` **Data Science ≠ writing code**  
It is **Problem Translation** — from Business Speak to Data Speak

`r fa('check-circle')` **Regression = conditional mean (descriptive)**  
$\hat{y} = E[y \mid \mathbf{x}]$ as market benchmark. Residuals show deviations.

`r fa('check-circle')` **Omitted Variable Bias**  
Missing variables bias all coefficients

`r fa('check-circle')` **Data Droughts**  
Algorithms are useless without the **right** data  
(`r Citet(bib, "thomasAIValueCreators2025")`: "Data Drought")

`r fa('check-circle')` **AI User vs. AI Value Creator**  
Commodity Data → operational efficiency.  
Proprietary Data → sustainable competitive advantage.
]

.pull-right[
<br>

### The CRISP-DM Cycle Today

.small[
| Phase | Result |
|-------|--------|
| Business Understanding | Underperformer = negative residual |
| Data Understanding | Sales right-skewed, ShelveLoc confounder |
| Data Preparation | log(Sales), dummy coding |
| Modeling | Simple → Multiple Regression |
| Evaluation | R² = ~0.50, OVB identified |
| Deployment | Managerial Memo with Make-or-Buy |
]

<br>
.blockquote[
"Regression does not give us truth. It gives us conditional averages — benchmarks against which to judge individual cases."
.tr[`r Citet(bib, "taddy_business_2019")`, S. 32]
]
]


---
class: left

.blockquote[Outlook]

## Outlook: Session 2 — Signal vs. Noise

.pull-left[
### The Open Question

Today we found patterns:

> *"Advertising has a positive effect on Sales"*  
> *"Good ShelveLoc increases revenue by X units"*

<br>

**But:**

> *How do we know these patterns are not pure **chance** in our sample of 400 branches?*

If we drew a different dataset tomorrow — would we get the same coefficients?
]

.pull-right[
<br>

### Next Session: Inference & Uncertainty

`r fa('dice')` **Bootstrapping** — Estimating uncertainty without normality assumptions

`r fa('chart-bar')` **Confidence intervals** — What is the "true" effect?

`r fa('exclamation-triangle')` **p-Hacking & "Only one test"** — Why Taddy warns against blind significance hunting

`r fa('brain')` **Decision logic under uncertainty** — When is a finding good enough for a million-dollar decision?

<br>
.blockquote[Signal vs. Noise — the title of the next block]
]

???
* Transition to Session 2: Inference is the core of "Critical Validation"
* Taddy: "Only one test" — the A/B-test principle
* Practical relevance: Should I really increase the advertising budget?


---

## References 

.ref-slide[
```{r refs, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
PrintBibliography(bib)
```
]
